nativeRange.habitat: Various habitat types from small upland headwater streams to main river channels and lakes; associated with submerged tree branches, roots, and floating logs; migrates to flooded areas during the wet season

decorPreferences: sand or fine smooth-gravel substrate to protect barbels
water-worn rocks and smooth boulders arranged to form caves and crevices
bogwood and driftwood providing hiding spots and visual barriers
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, Vallisneria) to reduce line-of-sight aggression
avoid sharp-edged decorations that can trap sub-ocular spines

diet.notes: Primarily carnivorous in practice; accepts quality sinking pellets, live and frozen bloodworms, Tubifex, Artemia, white mosquito larvae, chopped prawns, and mysis shrimp. Also consumes fresh vegetables (cucumber, courgette, melon, spinach) and will actively hunt and consume aquatic snails. Feed small portions 1–2 times daily; varied diet is important for long-term health.

goodWith: large fast-moving cyprinids (Devario, Danio, Rasbora species)
other large botiid loaches of similar size (Syncrossus helodes, Yasuhikotakia morleti)
avoidWith: small or slow-moving fish that may be intimidated or predated
long-finned or delicate fish (bettas, fancy guppies, angelfish)
fish smaller than approximately 6–8 cm that may be eaten

summary: A large, boldly striped Southeast Asian loach reaching 21 cm, the Tiger Loach is notable for its unusually high aggression among botiids. Must be kept in groups of 5+ in spacious, mature, well-oxygenated aquaria. Intermediate care; copper-sensitive; not for peaceful community tanks.
careNotes: Syncrossus hymenophysa is one of the larger and more aggressive members of the family Botiidae, and a significant step up from the more commonly kept clown loach or yoyo loach. The genus Syncrossus was separated from Botia by Kottelat in 2004, and older literature and trade still frequently use the synonym Botia hymenophysa. Adults reach 21 cm standard length (SL) and require a tank of at least 150 cm base length and approximately 300+ litres. A group of at least 5–6 individuals — preferably 8 or more — is essential: singly kept specimens become withdrawn, refuse food, or direct aggression at all tankmates, while a sufficiently large group allows a stable dominance hierarchy to form and distributes aggression among conspecifics. Unlike most loaches, S. hymenophysa is genuinely aggressive rather than merely boisterous — it will actively intimidate, chase, and injure smaller or slower tankmates. Suitable companions are large, fast-swimming cyprinids (Devario, Danio, larger Rasbora) and robust barbs that can hold their own; long-finned and slow-moving fish must be avoided entirely. Water quality is critical: these fish are highly intolerant of organic waste accumulation and should never be introduced to biologically immature aquaria. Maintain strong filtration (4–5x turnover per hour), good oxygenation, and weekly water changes of 30–50%. Preferred parameters are soft to moderately hard (gH/kH below 12) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.0–7.5) at 25–30°C, reflecting their Sumatran and Bornean riverine origin. Like all botiids, they possess sharp, retractable sub-ocular spines that can tangle fatally in nets; use smooth containers or bags for transfers and avoid sharp tank decorations. The species is susceptible to “skinny disease” caused by flagellate parasites (Spironucleus, Hexamita) and responds poorly to copper-based medications, which must be avoided entirely. Sand substrate of at least 3 cm protects sensory barbels. A tight-fitting lid is mandatory as this species will jump.
breedingNotes: Syncrossus hymenophysa has not been bred in captivity. The species is presumed to be a seasonal, migratory spawner in the wild, likely triggered by wet-season flooding and water chemistry changes. Females are noticeably stockier than males and are believed to be larger. No captive spawning protocol exists in the hobby literature as of 2026.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Syncrossus hymenophysa
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (150–210 mm SL), temperature (25–30°C), pH (5.0–7.5), hardness (18–215 ppm / approximately 0–12 dH), minimum tank dimensions (150 × 45 cm, ~304 L), diet, native range (Sumatra, Sarawak, West Kalimantan), habitat description, group size (5–6+), aggression notes, breeding status, and husbandry constraints including copper sensitivity and sub-ocular spines.

additional.name: FishBase — Syncrossus hymenophysa
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (21.0 cm SL), native range (Indonesia: Sumatra and Borneo; Malaya), habitat (freshwater demersal; lakes, rivers, streams near submerged wood), diet (worms, crustaceans, insects), taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes). Confirms FishBase max size aligns with SeriouslyFish upper bound.

additional.name: Loaches Online — Syncrossus hymenophysa
additional.notes: Specialist loach resource. Confirmed adult size (25 cm TL in trade), temperature (25–30°C), pH (7.0 or below), hardness (<12 dH), minimum tank length (4 ft+), diet (sinking pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, chopped prawns), group size (5+ minimum), aggression profile, substrate and hiding-place requirements. Also noted confusion with S. helodes and stripe-pattern distinction.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Tiger Botia Loach (Syncrossus hymenophysa)
additional.notes: Supplemental source. Confirmed pH (6.0–7.0), KH 8–12, gH <12, minimum 55-gallon (208 L) tank, lifespan ~7 years, group of 5+ same-size individuals, diet (bloodworms, worms, larvae), semi-aggressive temperament, no captive breeding records. Lists Java (historically) and Musi River basin, Sarawak, West Kalimantan, Pahang/Perak river systems in range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Syncrossus hymenophysa
additional.notes: Used to confirm genus-level taxonomy split (Syncrossus separated from Botia by Kottelat 2004), alternate common name Green Tiger Loach, distribution (Peninsular Malaysia, Borneo, Sumatra), and general size up to 10 in (25 cm). Also confirmed Least Concern IUCN status.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding on record. Presumed seasonal migratory spawner in the wild; females noticeably stockier and likely larger than males. No aquarium protocol exists.
